parsed.diagnosis: This job suffers from excessive file metadata operations, such as frequent open, stat, and close calls on many small files. Darshan profiling shows that metadata operations consume nearly 100% of the I/O time, indicating that the job spends most of its I/O runtime managing file metadata rather than transferring data. This inefficiency is typical when many small files are created or accessed individually, causing high overhead on the file system's metadata servers and severely limiting overall I/O performance.

recommendations.explanation: The job creates and closes a very large number of small files individually, causing excessive metadata operations that dominate I/O time. This can be optimized by reducing the number of files and batching metadata operations, for example by using a single shared file accessed collectively by all processes.

These issues result in a low total bandwidth of 7.64 MB/s over a 50-second runtime on 8 processes.

recommendations.explanation: The job issues many small write calls (~247 bytes), causing excessive syscall overhead and metadata operations. Aggregating these small writes into larger buffers (>=1MB) reduces syscall frequency and improves throughput.

parsed.diagnosis: The job uses suboptimal I/O interfaces by performing POSIX independent writes on shared files across multiple MPI ranks. This leads to inefficient coordination and serialization of I/O operations, causing low aggregate bandwidth (~15 MB/s) and increased metadata overhead (~12.7% of runtime). The presence of small I/O operations (9.16%) further exacerbates the inefficiency. Using collective MPI-IO operations with collective buffering can significantly improve parallel I/O performance by enabling better coordination and aggregation of I/O requests.

recommendations.explanation: The job currently uses POSIX independent writes where each rank seeks and writes independently to a shared file. This causes poor I/O coordination and low bandwidth. Switching to MPI-IO collective writes enables automatic coordination and collective buffering, improving parallel I/O efficiency.
recommendations.code_before:

write(fd, buf, size);
recommendations.code_after:
MPI_File_set_view(fh, my_offset, MPI_BYTE, filetype, "native", info);
MPI_File_write_all(fh, buf, size, MPI_BYTE, &status);

The very low metadata time ratio (0.0004) confirms that the bottleneck is in data access pattern rather than metadata overhead.

parsed.diagnosis: The job performs parallel writes using POSIX I/O on a shared single OST file, causing serialization and low throughput (68.53 MB/s) despite large sequential writes (64 KB). Excessive synchronization (likely fsync after every write) and lack of collective MPI-IO usage lead to underutilization of available bandwidth and redundant I/O traffic.

recommendations.explanation: Using POSIX I/O with independent seeks and writes on a shared file causes serialization on a single OST and low throughput. Switching to MPI-IO collective writes enables automatic coordination and collective buffering, improving parallel I/O efficiency.
recommendations.code_before:
lseek(fd, my_offset, SEEK_SET);
write(fd, buf, size);
recommendations.code_after:
MPI_File_set_view(fh, my_offset, MPI_BYTE, filetype, "native", info);
MPI_File_write_all(fh, buf, size, MPI_BYTE, &status);

fsync(fd);  /* single fsync at end */
